source_span_notes: The PDF table of contents was read from the local source file.
Arabic printed body pages map to physical PDF pages by adding 14.
Lecture spans include the homework section that closes the lecture when the table of contents places one before the next lecture or part.
References begin on printed page 197 / physical PDF page 211; the index begins on printed page 203 / physical PDF page 217.

parts.title: Part I: Introduction
parts.description: Symplectic forms, first examples, and cotangent bundles.

parts.title: Part II: Symplectomorphisms
parts.description: Lagrangian submanifolds, generating functions, and recurrence.

parts.title: Part III: Local Forms
parts.description: Isotopies, Moser's method, Darboux normal form, and Weinstein neighborhoods.

parts.title: Part IV: Contact Manifolds
parts.description: Contact forms, Reeb fields, and symplectization.

parts.title: Part V: Compatible Almost Complex Structures
parts.description: Compatible complex structures, triples, and Dolbeault decompositions.

parts.title: Part VI: Kahler Manifolds
parts.description: Complex manifolds, Kahler forms, Hodge restrictions, and examples.

parts.title: Part VII: Hamiltonian Mechanics
parts.description: Hamiltonian vector fields, variational principles, and Legendre transform.

parts.title: Part VIII: Moment Maps
parts.description: Group actions, Hamiltonian actions, and first moment-map examples.

parts.title: Part X: Moment Maps Revisited
parts.description: Gauge-theoretic moment maps, existence, uniqueness, and convexity.

parts.title: Part XI: Symplectic Toric Manifolds
parts.description: Delzant classification, construction, and Duistermaat-Heckman variation.

entries.label: Lecture 01
entries.title: Symplectic Forms
entries.focus: Builds the linear and manifold definition of a symplectic form from skew-symmetric bilinear algebra.
concepts: standard form for a skew map
entries.visual: matrix normal form and area-pair basis diagram
entries.lab: Classify random skew matrices by rank and check when the induced form is nondegenerate.
entries.proof: Track the standard-form theorem as a finite algorithm: split the kernel, pair directions, and test the matrix residual.

entries.label: Lecture 02
entries.title: Symplectic Form on the Cotangent Bundle
entries.focus: Introduces the tautological one-form and canonical symplectic form on a cotangent bundle.
sections: Tautological and canonical forms in coordinates
entries.visual: cotangent fibers and canonical-form grid
entries.lab: Compute alpha and omega on a simple cotangent bundle and verify coordinate-free pullback behavior.
entries.proof: Use the projection diagram to show why the same one-form appears in every coordinate chart.

entries.label: Lecture 03
entries.title: Lagrangian Submanifolds
entries.focus: Recasts symplectomorphisms as Lagrangian graphs and explains graphs of closed one-forms in cotangent bundles.
entries.visual: closed one-form graph and conormal slice
entries.lab: Check that the graph of df is Lagrangian and that a conormal bundle kills the tautological form.
entries.proof: Follow pullbacks of the tautological form through the graph inclusion until the condition becomes dmu=0.

entries.label: Lecture 04
entries.title: Generating Functions
entries.focus: Uses generating functions to manufacture Lagrangian graphs and hence symplectomorphisms.
entries.visual: contours of a generating function and the induced twist map
entries.lab: Vary a quadratic generating function and check the symplectic residual of the resulting map.
entries.proof: The proof view is a three-step route: exact graph, twist, projection test.

entries.label: Lecture 05
entries.title: Recurrence
entries.focus: Connects symplectic area preservation with periodic points, billiards, and Poincare recurrence.
entries.visual: billiard chords and recurrence histogram
entries.lab: Simulate a simple area-preserving rotation and estimate first return times to a window.
entries.proof: Treat recurrence as a measure bookkeeping theorem rather than as a pointwise convergence claim.

entries.label: Lecture 06
entries.title: Preparation for the Local Theory
entries.focus: Assembles isotopies, vector fields, tubular neighborhoods, and the homotopy formula needed for Moser arguments.
entries.visual: flowbox with normal disk fibers
entries.lab: Numerically compare a finite-flow deformation with the infinitesimal vector field that generates it.
entries.proof: Make Cartan's formula visible as a balance between derivative along a flow and exterior algebra operations.

entries.label: Lecture 07
entries.title: Moser Theorems
entries.focus: Develops Moser's trick as a controlled deformation of symplectic forms by solving for a vector field.
entries.visual: Moser density interpolation and vector-field arrows
entries.lab: Interpolate two equal-volume area forms and solve the one-dimensional Moser equation in a toy model.
entries.proof: The invariant tracker is the derivative of phi_t^* omega_t; the chosen vector field cancels it.

entries.label: Lecture 08
entries.title: Darboux-Moser-Weinstein Theory
entries.focus: Proves local symplectic normal forms and relates Lagrangian neighborhoods to cotangent models.
entries.visual: Darboux deformation ellipses and Lagrangian plane family
entries.lab: Track a path of nondegenerate forms and verify that the final chart has the standard matrix.
entries.proof: The proof is a local Moser argument with a linear algebra anchor at the point or Lagrangian.

entries.label: Lecture 09
entries.title: Weinstein Tubular Neighborhood Theorem
entries.focus: Applies local models to symplectic tubular neighborhoods, symplectomorphism groups, and fixed points.
sections: Tangent space to the symplectomorphism group
entries.visual: graph-near-diagonal fixed-point detector
entries.lab: Represent a near-identity map by its graph and test when it is Lagrangian in the twisted product.
entries.proof: Translate fixed points into intersections of a graph with the diagonal, then use local Lagrangian coordinates.

entries.label: Lecture 10
entries.title: Contact Forms
entries.focus: Introduces maximally nonintegrable hyperplane fields and first contact examples.
entries.visual: contact planes twisting along a curve
entries.lab: Evaluate alpha wedge d alpha in the standard R3 model and compare it with an integrable plane field.
entries.proof: The proof view is a Frobenius failure meter: planes twist enough that alpha wedge d alpha never vanishes.

entries.label: Lecture 11
entries.title: Contact Dynamics
entries.focus: Studies Reeb fields, symplectization, and the dynamical conjectures that motivate contact geometry.
entries.visual: Reeb flow helix and symplectization layer
entries.lab: Compute the Reeb vector field for a standard contact form and plot representative flow lines.
entries.proof: Reeb dynamics are isolated by two equations: alpha(R)=1 and i_R d alpha=0.

entries.label: Lecture 12
entries.title: Almost Complex Structures
entries.focus: Compares symplectic, metric, and almost-complex linear structures and the compatibility conditions between them.
entries.visual: compatibility matrix triangle
entries.lab: Construct J from the standard symplectic matrix and verify J^2=-I and positive g(u,v)=omega(u,Jv).
entries.proof: Compatibility is checked by closing a triangle: any two of omega, J, and g determine the third under positivity.

entries.label: Lecture 13
entries.title: Compatible Triples
entries.focus: Packages symplectic forms, metrics, and complex structures into mutually determining compatible triples.
entries.visual: omega-J-g reconstruction diagram
entries.lab: Perturb a compatible complex structure and measure whether the induced metric remains symmetric positive.
entries.proof: The invariant scaffold records which identities are algebraic and which depend on positivity.

entries.label: Lecture 14
entries.title: Dolbeault Theory
entries.focus: Introduces type decompositions of forms and the Dolbeault operators for almost complex and complex settings.
entries.visual: bigraded form lattice with operator arrows
entries.lab: Use symbolic z and zbar coordinates to check which simple functions are killed by dbar.
entries.proof: The proof diagram is a bigraded lattice: d splits into components whose squares encode integrability.

entries.label: Lecture 15
entries.title: Complex Manifolds
entries.focus: Moves from almost complex linear algebra to holomorphic charts and differential forms on complex manifolds.
entries.visual: two-chart atlas for CP1 and type decomposition
entries.lab: Compute the transition map between stereographic charts and verify holomorphic behavior away from overlap poles.
entries.proof: A chart-transition graph separates topological atlas data from holomorphic restrictions.

entries.label: Lecture 16
entries.title: Kahler Forms
entries.focus: Builds Kahler forms from compatible triples and potentials, including the Fubini-Study model.
entries.visual: Kahler potential contours and Fubini-Study density
entries.lab: Differentiate a radial potential and verify positivity of the associated Hermitian form.
entries.proof: The invariant tracker is closedness plus positivity: d omega=0 is automatic from a potential, while eigenvalues detect positivity.

entries.label: Lecture 17
entries.title: Compact Kahler Manifolds
entries.focus: Explains Hodge-theoretic restrictions and compares compact Kahler examples with symplectic nonexamples.
entries.visual: Hodge diamond and topology ledger
entries.lab: Build small Hodge diamonds and check the evenness constraints they impose on Betti numbers.
entries.proof: The proof view is a ledger of topological invariants forced by the Hodge decomposition.

entries.label: Lecture 18
entries.title: Hamiltonian Vector Fields
entries.focus: Defines Hamiltonian and symplectic vector fields and connects them to mechanics, brackets, and integrable systems.
entries.visual: phase portrait with Hamiltonian level sets
entries.lab: Integrate the harmonic oscillator and check that the Poisson bracket with its Hamiltonian vanishes.
entries.proof: The identity i_X omega=dH becomes visible because X_H is tangent to level curves of H.

entries.label: Lecture 19
entries.title: Variational Principles
entries.focus: Relates equations of motion to least action, Euler-Lagrange equations, and minimizing behavior.
entries.visual: path-action comparison and residual plot
entries.lab: Compare nearby polygonal paths and verify that the straight path has zero first-variation residual.
entries.proof: The proof scaffold turns a family of paths into an action curve and reads the derivative at zero.

entries.label: Lecture 20
entries.title: Legendre Transform
entries.focus: Uses strict convexity to pass between Lagrangian and Hamiltonian descriptions.
entries.visual: convex graph with tangent slopes and dual curve
entries.lab: Compute the Legendre transform of a quadratic-plus-quartic model and check Fenchel equality at the matching slope.
entries.proof: The invariant is dual contact: p=dL/dv selects the tangent line that supports the convex graph.

entries.label: Lecture 21
entries.title: Actions
entries.focus: Introduces one-parameter groups, Lie group actions, symplectic and Hamiltonian actions, and adjoint/coadjoint representations.
entries.visual: orbits and infinitesimal generators for a circle action
entries.lab: Plot a circle action on the plane and check that the infinitesimal generator preserves the standard form.
entries.proof: The proof view separates global group motion from the infinitesimal vector field it differentiates to.

entries.label: Lecture 22
entries.title: Hamiltonian Actions
entries.focus: Defines moment and comoment maps, previews reduction, and works through classical Hamiltonian action examples.
entries.visual: moment-map fibers for circle and torus actions
entries.lab: Verify d<mu,xi>=i_X omega for the standard circle action on C.
entries.proof: A moment map is a readable certificate that infinitesimal symmetry is Hamiltonian.

entries.label: Lecture 23
entries.title: The Marsden-Weinstein-Meyer Theorem
entries.focus: States and proves the main regular-level reduction theorem.
entries.visual: level set quotient and dimension count
entries.lab: Check the dimension formula for a free circle action and identify the kernel directions of the restricted form.
entries.proof: The proof is a kernel calculation: the directions killed by restricted omega are exactly the group orbit directions.

entries.label: Lecture 24
entries.title: Reduction
entries.focus: Explores Noether's principle, elementary reduction, product groups, other levels, and orbifold warnings.
entries.visual: conserved level slices and quotient fibers
entries.lab: Run a toy reduction of C2 by a circle and inspect how different levels change the reduced space.
entries.proof: The invariant scaffold keeps the conserved moment value fixed while quotienting redundant group directions.

entries.label: Lecture 25
entries.title: Moment Map in Gauge Theory
entries.focus: Reinterprets curvature of connections as a moment map for gauge-group action.
sections: Symplectic structure on the space of connections
entries.visual: discrete connection grid and curvature cells
entries.lab: Use a square lattice connection toy model and verify that curvature changes by a boundary term under a gauge shift.
entries.proof: The proof diagram upgrades finite-dimensional moment-map logic to an affine space of connections.

entries.label: Lecture 26
entries.title: Existence and Uniqueness of Moment Maps
entries.focus: Uses Lie algebra cohomology to organize obstructions and uniqueness for moment maps.
entries.visual: cocycle obstruction ledger
entries.lab: Compute a small Chevalley-Eilenberg-style coboundary table for an abelian example and check when ambiguity is constant.
entries.proof: The proof view isolates two questions: does each infinitesimal action have a Hamiltonian, and do the Hamiltonians respect brackets?

entries.label: Lecture 27
entries.title: Convexity
entries.focus: Presents convexity of moment-map images for torus actions and examples of effective actions.
entries.visual: fixed-point images and convex hull
entries.lab: Sample a torus action on CP2 and verify that the moment image lies inside the convex hull of fixed points.
entries.proof: Convexity is visualized as a global theorem forced by local normal forms near fixed points.

entries.label: Lecture 28
entries.title: Classification of Symplectic Toric Manifolds
entries.focus: States Delzant polytopes and the classification theorem for compact symplectic toric manifolds.
entries.visual: Delzant polygons with primitive normal checks
entries.lab: Compute adjacent normal determinants for triangle and square polytopes and flag a nonsmooth corner.
entries.proof: The proof scaffold turns geometry into combinatorics: compact toric manifolds are encoded by labeled smooth polytopes.

entries.label: Lecture 29
entries.title: Delzant Construction
entries.focus: Builds the toric manifold from a Delzant polytope via a quotient construction.
entries.visual: facet inequalities and quotient dimension flow
entries.lab: Construct the matrix of inward normals for a triangle and verify the quotient dimension count.
entries.proof: The proof diagram follows data through exact sequences, a moment level, and the final quotient.

entries.label: Lecture 30
entries.title: Duistermaat-Heckman Theorems
entries.focus: Describes how reduced symplectic volume varies polynomially with the moment-map level.
entries.visual: reduced-volume curve across moment levels
entries.lab: Compute a CP1/CP2 toy volume density and check its piecewise polynomial behavior.
entries.proof: The invariant tracker is the pushforward of symplectic volume along the moment map.
